PRs:
- [x] #55773 <!-- Add compat entry for `Base.donotdelete` -->
- [x] #56311 <!-- Fix `log_quasitriu` for internal scaling `s=0` -->
- [x] #56640 <!-- subtype: fast path for Type == TypeVar -->
- [x] #56784 <!-- fix `exp(weirdNaN)` -->
- [x] #56787 <!-- Fix partially_inline for unreachable -->
- [x] #43845 <!-- Add preference for version named manifest files -->

Need manual backport:
- [ ] #55405 <!-- handle unbound vars in NTuple fields -->
- [ ] #55744 <!-- fix #45494, error in ssa conversion with complex type decl -->
- [ ] #55890 <!-- Profile: fix order of fields in heapsnapshot & improve formatting -->
- [ ] #56789 <!-- Fix test report alignment -->

Contains multiple commits, manual intervention needed:
- [ ] #54738 <!-- serialization: fix relocatability bug -->
- [ ] #55306 <!-- AllocOpt: Fix stack lowering where alloca continas boxed and unboxed data -->
- [ ] #55729 <!-- Avoid confounding compilation side effects of `@time_imports` -->
- [ ] #55877 <!-- fix FileWatching designs and add workaround for a stat bug on Apple -->
- [ ] #56369 <!-- cache the `find_all_in_cache_path` call during parallel precompilation -->

Non-merged PRs with backport label:
- [ ] #56808 <!-- Export opaque closure constructor -->
- [ ] #56793 <!-- Extend `Base.rationalize` instead of defining new function `Base.MathConstants.rationalize` -->
- [ ] #56423 <!-- Fix and test lcm([1//2, 1//2]) == 1//1 -->
- [ ] #56411 <!-- xref `UnionAll` in the doc string of `where` -->
- [ ] #55958 <!-- also redirect JL_STDERR etc. when redirecting to devnull -->
- [ ] #55956 <!-- Make threadcall gc safe -->
- [ ] #55813 <!-- Check for conflicting `@ccallable` name before JIT registration -->
- [ ] #55534 <!-- Set stdlib sources as read-only during installation -->
- [ ] #55499 <!-- propagate the terminal's `displaysize` to the `IOContext` used by the REPL -->
- [ ] #55311 <!-- docs: Add headers for easier Core, Base and stdlib reference -->
- [ ] #55220 <!-- `isfile_casesensitive` fixes on Windows -->
- [ ] #55169 <!-- `propertynames` for SVD respects private argument -->
- [ ] #51479 <!-- prevent code loading from lookin in the versioned environment when building Julia -->
- [ ] #50813 <!-- More doctests for Sockets and capitalization fix -->
- [ ] #50157 <!-- improve docs for `@inbounds` and `Base.@propagate_inbounds` -->

Need manual backport:
- [ ] #51782 <!-- Fix remove-addrspaces pass in the presence of globals with addrspaces -->
- [ ] #55405 <!-- handle unbound vars in NTuple fields -->

- [ ] #56306 <!-- InteractiveUtils.jl: fixes issue where subtypes resolves bindings and causes deprecation warnings  -->
- [ ] #56511 <!-- The `info` in LAPACK calls should be a Ref instead of a Ptr -->

Contains multiple commits, manual intervention needed:
- [ ] #50832 <!-- Subtype: bug fix for bounds with deeper covariant var -->
- [ ] #54009 <!-- allow extensions to trigger from packages in [deps] -->
- [ ] #54738 <!-- serialization: fix relocatability bug -->
- [ ] #55306 <!-- AllocOpt: Fix stack lowering where alloca continas boxed and unboxed data -->
- [ ] #55720 <!-- Fix `pkgdir` for extensions -->
- [ ] #55729 <!-- Avoid confounding compilation side effects of `@time_imports` -->
- [ ] #55589 <!-- prevent loading other extensions when precompiling an extension -->
- [ ] #55877 <!-- fix FileWatching designs and add workaround for a stat bug on Apple -->
- [ ] #55886 <!-- irrationals: restrict assume effects annotations to known types -->
- [ ] #55870 <!-- fix infinite recursion in `promote_type` for `Irrational` -->
- [ ] #56252 <!-- REPL: fix brace detection when ' is used for transpose -->
- [ ] #56234 <!-- Revert "Extensions: make loading of extensions independent of what packages are in the sysimage (#52841) -->
- [ ] #56276 <!-- move time_imports and trace_* macros to Base but remain owned by InteractiveUtils -->
- [ ] #56254 <!-- REPL: don't complete str and cmd macros when the input matches the internal name like `r_` to `r"` -->
- [ ] #56280 <!-- Fix trampoline warning on x86 as well -->
- [ ] #42080 <!-- recommend explicit `using Foo: Foo, ...` in package code (was: "using considered harmful") -->
- [ ] #56369 <!-- cache the `find_all_in_cache_path` call during parallel precompilation -->
- [ ] #56441 <!-- Profile: mention `kill -s SIGUSR1 julia_pid` for Linux -->
- [ ] #55910 <!-- Prevent extensions from blocking parallel pre-compilation -->

Non-merged PRs with backport label:
- [ ] #56640 <!-- subtype: fast path for Type == TypeVar -->
- [ ] #56621 <!-- precompilepkgs: make the circular dep report prettier -->
- [ ] #56423 <!-- Fix and test lcm([1//2, 1//2]) == 1//1 -->
- [ ] #56411 <!-- xref `UnionAll` in the doc string of `where` -->
- [ ] #55958 <!-- also redirect JL_STDERR etc. when redirecting to devnull -->
- [ ] #55956 <!-- Make threadcall gc safe -->
- [ ] #55813 <!-- Check for conflicting `@ccallable` name before JIT registration -->
- [ ] #55534 <!-- Set stdlib sources as read-only during installation -->
- [ ] #55499 <!-- propagate the terminal's `displaysize` to the `IOContext` used by the REPL -->
- [ ] #55311 <!-- docs: Add headers for easier Core, Base and stdlib reference -->
- [ ] #55220 <!-- `isfile_casesensitive` fixes on Windows -->
- [ ] #55169 <!-- `propertynames` for SVD respects private argument -->
- [ ] #51479 <!-- prevent code loading from lookin in the versioned environment when building Julia -->
- [ ] #50813 <!-- More doctests for Sockets and capitalization fix -->

release-1.11"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-11-24T12:28:26Z","updated_at":"2024-11-24T19:40:32Z","closed_at":"2024-11-24T18:56:12Z","author_association":"MEMBER","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/56666","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56666","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56666.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/56666.patch","merged_at":"2024-11-24T18:56:12Z"},"body":"It is possible for an extension `ExtAB` to be loadable by one of its triggers, e.g. if `A` loads `B`. However, this loading is not supposed to happen during pre-compilation of `A`.

Getting this wrong means disagreeing with the scheduled pre-compile jobs (`A` is not scheduled to depend on or generate a cache file for `ExtAB` but accidentally attempts both) and leads to confusing errors about missing cache files.

We used to cover up this bad behavior w/ an erroneous cycle warning (fixed by #55910), but now we need to be sure this works.

a03c5cd49047ae9815f5242794537e9b5cc0a075 is the first bad commit
commit a03c5cd49047ae9815f5242794537e9b5cc0a075
Author: Cody Tapscott <84105208+topolarity@users.noreply.github.com>
Date:   Tue Mar 19 04:20:16 2024 -0400

    Fix handling of virtual exit node in `PostDomTree` (#53739)
    
    This is an alternative to https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/53642
    
    The `dom_edges()` for an exit block in the CFG are empty when computing
    the PostDomTree so the loop below this may not actually run. In that
    case, the right semidominator is the ancestor from the DFSTree, which is
    the "virtual" -1 block.
    
    This resolves half of the issue in
    https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53613:
    ```julia
    julia> let code = Any[
                   # block 1
                   GotoIfNot(Argument(2), 3),
                   # block 2
                   ReturnNode(Argument(3)),
                   # block 3 (we should visit this block)
                   Expr(:call, throw, "potential throw"),
                   ReturnNode(), # unreachable
               ]
               ir = make_ircode(code; slottypes=Any[Any,Bool,Bool])
               visited = BitSet()
               @test !Core.Compiler.visit_conditional_successors(CC.LazyPostDomtree(ir), ir, #=bb=#1) do succ::Int
                   push!(visited, succ)
                   return false
               end
               @test 2 ∈ visited
               @test 3 ∈ visited
           end
    Test Passed
    ```
    
    This needs some tests (esp. since I don't think we have any DomTree
    tests at all right now), but otherwise should be good to go.

For example, in my Metal.jl dev environment, I have `Manifest.toml`, `Manifest-v1.11.toml`, and `Manifest-v1.12.toml`.

Maybe if there's a manifest that matches the next-highest priority setting, select that?

For example, if I start Julia with 
```bash
$ JULIAUP_CHANNEL=rc julia --project=.
```
in an environment where my default channel is `release`, and that I have `Manifest.toml`, `Manifest-v1.11.toml`, and `Manifest-v1.12.toml` in that folder, it would use `Manifest-v1.11.toml` since the next priority is `JULIAUP_CHANNEL`, and it's set to `rc`.

Calling 
```bash
$ julia --project=.
```
would launch v1.10 because that's the default channel.

I'm not exactly sure this should interact with the Julia Manifest searching priority order (listed in my other review comment), but it's worth figuring suggestion by @tecosaur, also use the heavy right box drawing character.  Can be tested with:

```julia
function test_progress(n, dt)
   io = stdout
   bar = Pkg.MiniProgressBars.MiniProgressBar(; indent=1, header = "Downloading packages", color = Base.info_color(),
                                    mode=:int, always_reprint=true)
   bar.max = n
   for i in 1:n
      bar.current = i
      Pkg.MiniProgressBars.show_progress(io, bar)
      sleep(dt)
   end
end
```

Doing something like `test_progress(1000, 0.001)` shows some difference vs the old behavior.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/4038/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/4038/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/comments/2398020954","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/pull/4038#issuecomment-2398020954","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/4038","id":2398020954,"node_id":"IC_kwDOBOhtSc6O7uVa","user":{"login":"christiangnrd","id":28689358,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4Njg5MzU4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/28689358?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd","html_url":"https://github.com/christiangnrd","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-07T22:18:26Z","updated_at":"2024-10-07T22:18:26Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"While playing around with this I noticed that the double space before the progress bar is still present even after #4008. bar","user":{"login":"christiangnrd","id":28689358,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4Njg5MzU4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/28689358?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd","html_url":"https://github.com/christiangnrd","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-10-14T00:33:18Z","updated_at":"2024-10-14T00:33:18Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"As raised by @davidanthoff on slack, while the new progress bars look great, they can be harder to see due to the differentiation between both sides being mostly colour. This problem is exacerbated when Julia is launched with no colour output.

Current progress bar in VSCode (examples 1/2):
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9b99640e-6fe5-4360-9add-5582fb8ff0a6)

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a3288323-997e-42b8-9154-9fd067bed7ba)

I looked at how PIP deals with no colour since this design was inspired off theirs, they replace the unfilled side with white space to ensure good contrast.

Example 3:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d25057a6-8922-4621-bd34-ff97879dccf2)

Another idea I had would be to keep using the heavy character variant on the left, but use the light variant on the right. That would add even more differentiation while still looking similar to the new style.

Examples 4/5:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/659c3875-a41b-4290-b219-c0af038bcee7)
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e0e52f12-970a-4072-91ec-b63ed85e4024)

Personally, I think the no colour option should use whitespace on the right (example 3), while for a colour terminal, I don't know if I prefer the current status bar or my suggestion (1 or 4).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/4046/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/4046/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

raised by @davidanthoff on slack, while the new progress bars look great, they can be harder to see due to the differentiation between both sides being mostly colour. This problem is exacerbated when Julia is launched with no colour output.

Current progress bar in VSCode (examples 1/2):
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/9b99640e-6fe5-4360-9add-5582fb8ff0a6)

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a3288323-997e-42b8-9154-9fd067bed7ba)

I looked at how PIP deals with no colour since this design was inspired off theirs, they replace the unfilled side with white space to ensure good contrast.

Example 3:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/d25057a6-8922-4621-bd34-ff97879dccf2)

Another idea I had would be to keep using the heavy character variant on the left, but use the light variant on the right. That would add even more differentiation while still looking similar to the new style.

Examples 4/5:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/659c3875-a41b-4290-b219-c0af038bcee7)
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/e0e52f12-970a-4072-91ec-b63ed85e4024)

Personally, I think the no colour option should use whitespace on the right (example 3), while for a colour terminal, I don't know if I prefer the current status bar or my suggestion (1 or attempt at resolving #1068.

The source of the panic seems to be an assertion that was added purely to remove a compiler warning, so I turned it into a warning because I don't think an empty version field should prevent updating to a latest PR.

TODO: If people decide this is an acceptable solution, I'll improve the warning message with potential causes.

One cause is when an installed channel has an empty version field due to not being able to get the version number from a non-codesigned executable on macOS https://github.com/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/1068#issuecomment-2409141076. It could also be that a particular PR was merged So if the underlying issue at the moment is that pr channels just don't work, then maybe we should (in the very short run) disable that feature on MacOS, and then try to properly fix #1000.
> 
> But I think we should not just start to ignore these empty versions at this place here, instead we should probably just abort installation of a PR channel if we can't launch the executable to extract the version in the first place.

While I agree #1086 is a much better solution. I think that this PR would be a better very-short-term solution than disabling the feature for mac users because it prevents a crash when trying to update, and still allows to download/update a PR branch. +## Execution latency, package loading and package precompiling time
+
+### Reducing time to first plot etc.
+
+The first time a julia method is called it (and any methods it calls, or ones that can be statically determined) will be
+compiled. The [`@time`](@ref) macro family illustrates this.
+
+```
+julia> foo() = rand(2,2) * rand(2,2)
+foo (generic function with 1 method)
+
+julia> @time @eval foo();
+  0.252395 seconds (1.12 M allocations: 56.178 MiB, 2.93% gc time, 98.12% compilation time)
+
+julia> @time @eval foo();
+  0.000156 seconds (63 allocations: 2.453 KiB)
+```
+
+Note that `@time @eval` is better for measuring compilation time because without [@eval](@ref), some compilation may
+already be done before timing starts.
+
+When developing a package, you may be able to improve the experience of your users with *precompilation*,
+so that when they use the package the code they use is already compiled. Discovered because of new tests.
```
Test Failed at /Users/christian/.julia/juliaup/julia-nightly/share/julia/stdlib/v1.12/REPL/test/repl.jl:1971
  Expression: prompt == "(@v$(VERSION.major).$(VERSION.minor)) pkg> "
   Evaluated: "(v1.12) pkg> " == "(@v1.12) pkg> "
```

Seems like `projname(project_file::String)` only checks `abspath` instead of `safe_realpath` when comparing `envdir` and `project_file` https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/fc9f1470458ad6bdeb1f56c1150d651814a0a164/stdlib/REPL/src/Pkg_beforeload.jl#L89-L94

Repl walkthrough of my investigation:
```ChrichriMBP:~$ j +nightly
               _
   _       _ _(_)_     |  Documentation: https://docs.julialang.org
  (_)     | (_) (_)    |
   _ _   _| |_  __ _   |  Type "?" for help, "]?" for Pkg help.
  | | | | | | |/ _` |  |
  | | |_| | | | (_| |  |  Version 1.12.0-DEV.1238 (2024-09-23)
 _/ |\__'_|_|_|\__'_|  |  Commit 0fade450a18 (0 days old master)
|__/                   |

julia> withenv("JULIA_DEPOT_PATH" => mktempdir(), "JULIA_LOAD_PATH" => nothing) do
              run(`$(Base.julia_cmd()[1]) --startup-file=no`)
          end
               _
   _       _ _(_)_     |  Documentation: https://docs.julialang.org
  (_)     | (_) (_)    |
   _ _   _| |_  __ _   |  Type "?" for help, "]?" for Pkg help.
  | | | | | | |/ _` |  |
  | | |_| | | | (_| |  |  Version 1.12.0-DEV.1238 (2024-09-23)
 _/ |\__'_|_|_|\__'_|  |  Commit 0fade450a18 (0 days old master)
|__/                   |

julia> using REPL; REPL.Pkg_promptf()
[ Info: Precompiling REPL [3fa0cd96-eef1-5676-8a61-b3b8758bbffb] 
"(v1.12) pkg> "

julia> depot = Base.DEPOT_PATH |> first
"/var/folders/4g/lnkpkf3s4rxd_wbl8vwnqs4r0000gn/T/jl_KEaptd"

julia> envdir = joinpath(depot, "environments")
"/var/folders/4g/lnkpkf3s4rxd_wbl8vwnqs4r0000gn/T/jl_KEaptd/environments"

julia> project_file = REPL.find_project_file()
"/private/var/folders/4g/lnkpkf3s4rxd_wbl8vwnqs4r0000gn/T/jl_KEaptd/environments/v1.12/Project.toml"

julia> startswith(abspath(project_file), abspath(envdir)) # Line 91 in Pkg_beforeload.jl
false

Potential options I came up with:
1. Treat them like any invalid channel. (easy option)
2. Look online to see if it’s a valid pr with an uploaded build and show the uninstalled channel message. (Would have to connect to the internet for a step that doesn’t typically need to)
3. Find a concise way to explain that it may or may not be a valid channel that happens to be uninstalled. (Every phrasing I come up with for this feels sloppy and drawn out)

Any ideas?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/comments/2229892967/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I'm moving the conversation here as I've decided #848 won't try to fix PRs too.

>I was working on the merge conflicts in rebasing #848 to main earlier and it got me wondering how  PR channels should be considered. 
>
>Potential options I came up with:
>1. Treat them like any invalid channel. (easy option)
>2. Look online to see if it’s a valid pr with an uploaded build and show the uninstalled channel message. (Would have to connect to the internet for a step that doesn’t typically need to)
>3. Find a concise way to explain that it may or may not be a valid channel that happens to be uninstalled. (Every phrasing I come up with for this feels sloppy and drawn out)
>
>Any ideas?
>
>_Originally posted by @christiangnrd in https://github.com/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/820#issuecomment-2229892967_

>It seems reasonable to me to only know about the PRs available as of the last update. If you want a new PR do an update?
>
>_Originally posted by @StefanKarpinski in https://github.com/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/820#issuecomment-2230651453_

@StefanKarpinski I don't believe the local database has a list of available PRs, so it would have to try to download at least the header to check that that PR is available.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/comments/2231534771/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

https://github.com/JuliaLang/juliaup

We take privacy seriously, and do not perform any automated error collection. In order to improve the software, we rely on people to submit reports.

Thank you kindly!"

I attach the report below, as requested.:

"

Pkg is now a pkgimage and can load slowly on slower machines, which is a bit frustrating in the repl.

This makes the repl immediately switch to a dummy prompt that looks like Pkg mode to allow the user to keep typing while Pkg loads. During which the keymap is disabled.

It works best if julia has >1 thread, otherwise typing stalls during Pkg load.
Side note, it might make sense if by default julia in interactive mode started with at least `-t1,1`.

If Pkg takes longer to load than the user to type the command and press return, then the UX isn't great as it won't do anything.

Opening as a draft as the threading limitation is a bit awkward, and there may be a better approach to achieve the same UX.



https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/1694067/1bf17323-441a-4db2-8a3b-4d571eac622f

on https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54594 based on https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54594#issuecomment-2148132537 and review below

- Implements dummy pkg prompt as an actual repl mode
- Makes premature return execute once loaded

go faster"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-04-27T00:57:01Z","updated_at":"2024-04-27T14:13:26Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'm observing mysterious slowdowns when using different integer types. As a MWE, consider the following two functionally equivalent functions `f1` and `f2`:
```
function inc_index(v::NTuple{N,T}, i) where {N,T}
# increments the i-th coordinate of v by 1
    t = ntuple(Val(N)) do k
        ntuple(Val(N)) do j
            T(j == k)
        end
    end
    @inbounds v .+ t[i]
end

function f1(v, pp)
    for p in pp
        v = inc_index(v, isodd(p)+1)
    end
    v
end

function f2(v, pp)
    foldl(pp; init = v) do w, p
        inc_index(w, isodd(p)+1)
    end
end
```
On Julia 1.10.2 I get similar benchmarks for `f1` and `f2` with `Int16`, but 20x slowdown for `f1` and `Int32`.
```
julia> pp = collect(1:1000);

julia> T = Int16; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f1($v, $pp)
544.000 ns

julia> T = Int16; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f2($v, $pp)
485.576 ns

julia> T = Int32; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f1($v, $pp)
14.217 μs

julia> T = Int32; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f2($v, $pp)
490.224 ns
```
With Julia 1.11.0-beta1 I get slowdowns for both `f1` and `f2` when using `Int32`:
```
julia> T = Int16; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f1($v, $pp)
543.553 ns

julia> T = Int16; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f2($v, $pp)
409.563 ns

julia> T = Int32; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f1($v, $pp)
14.214 μs

julia> T = Int32; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f2($v, $pp)
14.074 μs
```
```

Commit bd47eca2c8a (2024-03-01 10:14 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:

PR was formed by running a bunch of global find-and-replaces
```
find . -name *.jl -exec sed -i 's/inaccessiblememonly/inaccessible_mem_only/g' {} \;
```
And manually updating the public `@assume_effects` macro to continue accepting the old symbols as arguments.

From discussion on Zulip (https://julialang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/137791-general/topic/About.2Ejl/near/436262421)

This seems worth doing in the light of the inconsistency between `inaccessiblememonly` and `effect_free` and the fact that effect analysis is slowly becoming public.

Very open to adjusting the scope of this PR/how much we rename. I leaned pretty far into making all the effect analysis snake case in this first draft to see what folks think and to avoid having to make PR was formed by running a bunch of global find-and-replaces
```
find . -name *.jl -exec sed -i 's/inaccessiblememonly/inaccessible_mem_only/g' {} \;
```
And manually updating the public `@assume_effects` macro to continue accepting the old symbols as arguments.

From discussion on Zulip (https://julialang.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/137791-general/topic/About.2Ejl/near/436262421)

This seems worth doing in the light of the inconsistency between `inaccessiblememonly` and `effect_free` and the fact that effect analysis is slowly becoming public.

Very open to adjusting the scope of this PR/how much we rename. I leaned pretty far into making all the effect analysis snake case in this first draft to see what folks think and to avoid having to make documents the failure cases of various trigonometric functions provided in Base, but is not exhaustive. In particular, there are still undocumented failure cases of the versions of these functions taking matrices, but this can be added at a later date. The failure cases where found with PropCheck.jl, checking if the property `x -> f(x) isa T` for the functions `f` in question and the types `(Int8, Int16, Int32, Int64, Float16, Float32, Float64)` holds (i.e., doesn't error and returns `true`). I can give a script reproducing the failures if requested.

I'd expect CI to be happy, unless someone somewhere is checking for the exact error message when a `DomainError` is thrown - some of the functions in here had a really bare bones error message, which I've also aligned with the formulation we use in other places.

I'm not 100% sure who best to review this, but since I know @oscardssmith and @stevengj have done plenty of numeric work on these functions in the past, have a ping latest master (52fc79696e0b08fe1793a001daaefd1d91d59f0c) the number of methods for a function for which we redefine the same method multiple times increases steadily:
```julia
julia> f() = nothing
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> f() = nothing
f (generic function with 2 methods)

julia> f() = nothing
f (generic function with 3 methods)

julia> methods(f)
# 3 methods for generic function "f" from Main:
 [1] f()
     @ REPL[1]:1
 [2] f()
     @ REPL[1]:1
 [3] f()
     @ REPL[1]:1

julia> length(methods(f)) == 1
false
```
`length(methods(f))` would be a good test for the resolution of this bug, or for a bisection script (can't run it myself now).  This doesn't happen in v1.10 nor v1.11, so it should be somewhat ```julia
x = zeros(2)
A = rand(2, 2)
l = x * A[1:1, :]
resize!(x, 3)
```
with error message
```
ERROR: cannot resize array with shared data
Stacktrace:
 [1] _growend!
   @ ./array.jl:1070 [inlined]
 [2] resize!
   @ ./array.jl:1313 [inlined]
 [3] top-level scope
 ```

It's related to #43507 and #33143. 

The difference between the existing issues is that from a user perspective, there is no in-place operation done on `x`. Therefore, it was surprising for me that `x` loses it's data. 

(One can avoid the error by using `view(x,:) * A[1:1, :]` or `x * A[1,:]'`.)



The code was run on `Julia 1.10`. (I can provide version info etc, but it seems not that relevant.)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53191/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53191/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1927390717","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53191#issuecomment-1927390717","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53191","id":1927390717,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5y4aX9","user":{"login":"christiangnrd","id":28689358,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4Njg5MzU4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/28689358?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd","html_url":"https://github.com/christiangnrd","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-02-05T16:31:28Z","updated_at":"2024-02-05T16:31:28Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"This Debian terminfo directory default to find_terminfo_file","user":{"login":"christiangnrd","id":28689358,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4Njg5MzU4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/28689358?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd","html_url":"https://github.com/christiangnrd","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":11,"created_at":"2023-10-21T17:15:50Z","updated_at":"2024-02-06T17:56:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/51809","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51809","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51809.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51809.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Without this, `Base.current_terminfo` returns `TermInfo(String[]; 0 flags, 0 numbers, 0 strings)` because it cannot find the correct terminfo files. In the regular terminal, there was basic color and formatting functionality because `$TERM`s starting with `xterm` are treated specially, but in a tmux session, where the `$TERM = tmux-256color`, there was no color at all.

This also has the side-effect of enabling strikethrough and italics in the default gnome terminal emulator.

The readme found in `/etc/terminfo/README` (as mentioned in `terminfo(5) "Fetching Compiled Descriptions"`) says the following:

```
/etc/terminfo/README 
This directory is for system-local terminfo descriptions. By default,
ncurses will search ${HOME}/.terminfo first, then /etc/terminfo (this
directory), then /lib/terminfo, and last not least /usr/share/terminfo.
```

I believe the manual might be different depending on the distro.

```julia
help?> Sys.total_memory
  Sys.total_memory()

  Get the total memory in RAM (including that which is currently used) in bytes.
  This amount may be constrained, e.g., by Linux control groups. For the
  unconstrained amount, see Sys.physical_memory().
```
However `Sys.physical_memory()` does not exist. Replaced by `Sys.total_physical_memory()`?

julia> versioninfo()

{"action":"opened","number":52932,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/52932","id":1681695183,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5kPKHP","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52932","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52932.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/52932.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52932","number":52932,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Fix string output of `Base.format_bytes` when `binary=false`","user":{"login":"christiangnrd","id":28689358,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4Njg5MzU4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/28689358?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd","html_url":"https://github.com/christiangnrd","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Introduced in [50572](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50572)

Before:
```
julia> Base.format_bytes(1, binary=false)
"1 "

julia> Base.format_bytes(11, binary=false)
"11 s"
```

This PR:
```
julia> Base.format_bytes(1, binary=false)
"1 byte"

julia> Base.format_bytes(11, binary=false)
"11 bytes"
```

On master:
```
julia> begin # Passes on its own
           @test (@allocations "a") == 0
       end
Test Passed

julia> begin # Passes in this situation
           [i for i in 0:3]
           @test (@allocations "a") == 0
       end
Test Passed

julia> begin # Fails here
           [1 for i in 0:3]
           @test (@allocations "a") == 0
       end
Test Failed at REPL[12]:3
  Expression: #= REPL[12]:3 =# @allocations("a") == 0
   Evaluated: 1 == 0

ERROR: There was an error during testing

julia> function broken() # Works when in a function
           [1 for i in 0:3]
           @test (@allocations "a") == 0
       end; broken()
Test Passed
```

Before:
```
julia> Base.format_bytes(1, binary=false)
"1 "

julia> Base.format_bytes(11, binary=false)
"11 s"
```

This PR:
```
julia> Base.format_bytes(1, binary=false)
"1 byte"

julia> Base.format_bytes(11, binary=false)
"11 bytes"
```

 # Using Valgrind with Julia
 
-[Valgrind](https://valgrind.org/) is a tool for memory debugging, memory leak detection, and profiling.
+[Valgrind](https://valgrind.org/) is a tool for memory-debugging, memory leak detection, and profiling.
  This section describes things to keep in mind when using Valgrind to debug memory issues with
 Julia.
 
 ## General considerations
 
-By default, Valgrind assumes that there is no self modifying code in the programs it runs.  This
+By default, Valgrind assumes that, there is no self modifying code in the programs it runs.  This
 assumption works fine in most instances but fails miserably for a just-in-time compiler like
 `julia`.  For this reason it is crucial to pass `--smc-check=all-non-file` to `valgrind`, else
 code may crash or behave unexpectedly (often in subtle ways).
 
-In some cases, to better detect memory errors using Valgrind it can help to compile `julia` with
-memory pools disabled.  The compile-time flag `MEMDEBUG` disables memory pools in Julia, and
+In some cases, to better detect memory errors using Valgrind, it can help to compile `julia` with

{"action":"opened","number":51809,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/51809","id":1567826578,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5dcyKS","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51809","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51809.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51809.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51809","number":51809,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Add Debian terminfo directory default to find_terminfo_file","user":{"login":"christiangnrd","id":28689358,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4Njg5MzU4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/28689358?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd","html_url":"https://github.com/christiangnrd","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Without this, `Base.current_terminfo` returns `TermInfo(String[]; 0 flags, 0 numbers, 0 strings)` because it cannot find the correct terminfo files. In the regular terminal, there was basic color and formatting functionality because `$TERM`s starting with `xterm` are treated specially, but in a tmux session, where the `$TERM = tmux-256color`, there was no color at all.

This also has the side-effect of enabling strikethrough and italics in the default gnome terminal emulator.

The readme found in `/etc/terminfo/README` (as mentioned in `terminfo(5) "Fetching Compiled Deescriptions"`) says the following:

```
/etc/terminfo/README 
This directory is for system-local terminfo descriptions. By default,
ncurses will search ${HOME}/.terminfo first, then /etc/terminfo (this
directory), then /lib/terminfo, and last not least /usr/share/terminfo.
```

I believe the manual might be different depending on the distro.

Debian terminfo directory default to find_terminfo_file","user":{"login":"christiangnrd","id":28689358,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4Njg5MzU4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/28689358?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd","html_url":"https://github.com/christiangnrd","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-10-21T17:15:50Z","updated_at":"2023-10-21T17:18:34Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/51809","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51809","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51809.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51809.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Without this, `Base.current_terminfo` returns `TermInfo(String[]; 0 flags, 0 numbers, 0 strings)` because it cannot find the correct terminfo files. In the regular terminal, there was basic color and formatting functionality because `$TERM`s starting with `xterm` are treated specially, but in a tmux session, where the `$TERM = tmux-256color`, there was no color at all.

This also has the side-effect of enabling strikethrough and italics in the default gnome terminal emulator.

The readme found in `/etc/terminfo/README` (as mentioned in `terminfo(5) "Fetching Compiled Deescriptions"`) says the following:

```
/etc/terminfo/README 
This directory is for system-local terminfo descriptions. By default,
ncurses will search ${HOME}/.terminfo first, then /etc/terminfo (this
directory), then /lib/terminfo, and last not least /usr/share/terminfo.
```

I believe the manual might be different depending on the distro.

find_terminfo_file","user":{"login":"christiangnrd","id":28689358,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4Njg5MzU4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/28689358?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd","html_url":"https://github.com/christiangnrd","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2023-10-21T17:15:50Z","updated_at":"2023-10-21T17:43:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/51809","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51809","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51809.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/51809.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Without this, `Base.current_terminfo` returns `TermInfo(String[]; 0 flags, 0 numbers, 0 strings)` because it cannot find the correct terminfo files. In the regular terminal, there was basic color and formatting functionality because `$TERM`s starting with `xterm` are treated specially, but in a tmux session, where the `$TERM = tmux-256color`, there was no color at all.

This also has the side-effect of enabling strikethrough and italics in the default gnome terminal emulator.

The readme found in `/etc/terminfo/README` (as mentioned in `terminfo(5) "Fetching Compiled Descriptions"`) says the following:

```
/etc/terminfo/README 
This directory is for system-local terminfo descriptions. By default,
ncurses will search ${HOME}/.terminfo first, then /etc/terminfo (this
directory), then /lib/terminfo, and last not least /usr/share/terminfo.
```

I believe the manual might be different depending on the distro.

On Debian (raspberrypiOS) and Ubuntu 23.04, the manual has the following:

```
   Fetching Compiled Descriptions
       The  ncurses  library  searches  for  terminal  descriptions in several places.  It uses only the first description
       found.  The library has a compiled-in list of places to search which can be overridden  by  environment  variables.
       Before starting to search, ncurses eliminates duplicates in its search list.

       •   If  the  environment  variable TERMINFO is set, it is interpreted as the pathname of a directory containing the
           compiled description you are working on.  Only that directory is searched.

       •   If TERMINFO is not set, ncurses will instead look in the directory $HOME/.terminfo for a compiled description.

       •   Next, if the environment variable TERMINFO_DIRS is set, ncurses will interpret the contents of that variable as
           a list of colon-separated directories (or database files) to be searched.

           An empty directory name (i.e., if the variable begins or ends with a colon, or contains adjacent colons) is in‐
           terpreted as the system location /etc/terminfo.

       •   Finally, ncurses searches these compiled-in locations:

           •   a list of directories (no default value), and

           •   the system terminfo directory, /etc/terminfo (the compiled-in default).
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1773874705/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

merge"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":57,"created_at":"2023-05-01T18:01:45Z","updated_at":"2023-09-03T15:35:30Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/49586","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49586","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49586.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49586.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Styled content is hard to deal with. This contribution aims to drastically improve that. ## Current holdups - Lack of feedback - Lack of documentation - Lack of tests ## New public API - `StyledString` - `StyledChar` - `Base.Face` - `addface!` - `@S_str` - `styledstring` should anything else be added? ## Motivation Julia already treats the REPL experience better than most other languages out there (🐍), and I think it's great how often I see the REPL as a selling point of Julia. However... styling is hard. Across `Base` and the package ecosystem we have a painful mixture of raw ANSI codes and an unsightly pile of `printstyled` lines. This isn't just ugly, this leads to reoccurring issues with incorrectly computed string lengths/padding and unterminated styling codes (e.g. #37568 and #45521). We can do better than this. Rethinking our approach to styled content allows us to do away with this class of bugs/headaches altogether, and gain a number of shiny new features at the same time. Hy hope is that the capabilities introduced by this PR will lead to a much more robust approach to string styling, and by making it easier to produce well-styled content even more beautiful REPL experiences across the board. ## Rundown of changes An `StyledString` type is introduced that effectively sits on top of other string types. It wraps another `AbstractString` but then separately stores a list of attributes applied to particular regions of the wrapped string. Regions are represented by a `UnitRange{Int}`, and attributes as a `Pair{Symbol, Any}`. This effectively creates "content" and "attributes" layers, allowing both to be handled much better (conveniently and robustly) than when mixed together. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛. An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead. To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉). The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages: - It makes it easy to reference faces by their name - Packages can easily add new faces - It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`) Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435. To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`. Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient. #### Currently unimplemented - `replace` - property removal ## Usage examples The example from the `@S_str` docs ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492934-d3299633-36fe-4a21-8321-83e24d8c4a6f.png) Easy nested styling ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235493196-753f50e4-3acc-4821-9146-8c1aef5e6cd1.png) No more issues with unterminated ANSI codes ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235494848-eccefbda-f943-4c1d-85a0-dbd2297b9217.png) String functions operate on the unstyled content, while preserving styling where possible ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496767-1d537db9-befc-4ee6-b482-e91778e32ceb.png) ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496290-d3b6bb79-9c33-4dce-bf6f-b20d696cc5b2.png) Styled text will actually automatically fall back to the un-styled form when printed to an `io` with `:color != true`. ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496566-330a621d-e0d9-4b36-8714-e9d225e89ef7.png) Incrementally styling a string, and changing face definitions on-the-fly (note: this uses currently "private API" functions) ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235499614-ce8d5aca-a561-4280-979e-d6c176efd1df.png) ## Fancier merge"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":59,"created_at":"2023-05-01T18:01:45Z","updated_at":"2023-09-03T19:12:09Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/49586","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49586","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49586.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/49586.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Styled content is hard to deal with. This contribution aims to drastically improve that. ## Current holdups - Lack of feedback - Lack of documentation - Lack of tests ## New public API - `StyledString` - `StyledChar` - `Base.Face` - `addface!` - `@S_str` - `styledstring` should anything else be added? ## Motivation Julia already treats the REPL experience better than most other languages out there (🐍), and I think it's great how often I see the REPL as a selling point of Julia. However... styling is hard. Across `Base` and the package ecosystem we have a painful mixture of raw ANSI codes and an unsightly pile of `printstyled` lines. This isn't just ugly, this leads to reoccurring issues with incorrectly computed string lengths/padding and unterminated styling codes (e.g. #37568 and #45521). We can do better than this. Rethinking our approach to styled content allows us to do away with this class of bugs/headaches altogether, and gain a number of shiny new features at the same time. Hy hope is that the capabilities introduced by this PR will lead to a much more robust approach to string styling, and by making it easier to produce well-styled content even more beautiful REPL experiences across the board. ## Rundown of changes An `StyledString` type is introduced that effectively sits on top of other string types. It wraps another `AbstractString` but then separately stores a list of attributes applied to particular regions of the wrapped string. Regions are represented by a `UnitRange{Int}`, and attributes as a `Pair{Symbol, Any}`. This effectively creates "content" and "attributes" layers, allowing both to be handled much better (conveniently and robustly) than when mixed together. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛. An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead. To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉). The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages: - It makes it easy to reference faces by their name - Packages can easily add new faces - It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`) Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435. To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`. Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient. #### Currently unimplemented - `replace` - property removal ## Usage examples The example from the `@S_str` docs ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492934-d3299633-36fe-4a21-8321-83e24d8c4a6f.png) Easy nested styling ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235493196-753f50e4-3acc-4821-9146-8c1aef5e6cd1.png) No more issues with unterminated ANSI codes ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235494848-eccefbda-f943-4c1d-85a0-dbd2297b9217.png) String functions operate on the unstyled content, while preserving styling where possible ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496767-1d537db9-befc-4ee6-b482-e91778e32ceb.png) ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496290-d3b6bb79-9c33-4dce-bf6f-b20d696cc5b2.png) Styled text will actually automatically fall back to the un-styled form when printed to an `io` with `:color != true`. ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496566-330a621d-e0d9-4b36-8714-e9d225e89ef7.png) Incrementally styling a string, and changing face that's been implemented using `StyledString`s and `JuliaSyntax` that would be a massive pain to do via `printstyled` et al. Documentation: https://docs.julialang.org

content is hard to deal with. This contribution aims to drastically improve that.

## Current holdups

- Lack of feedback
- Lack of documentation
- Lack of tests

## New public API

- `StyledString`
- `StyledChar`
- `Base.Face`
- `addface!`
- `@S_str`
- `styledstring`

should anything else be added?

## Motivation

Julia already treats the REPL experience better than most other languages out there (🐍), and I think it's great how often I see the REPL as a selling point of Julia.

However... styling is hard. Across `Base` and the package ecosystem we have a painful mixture of raw ANSI codes and an unsightly pile of `printstyled` lines. This isn't just ugly, this leads to reoccurring issues with incorrectly computed string lengths/padding and unterminated styling codes (e.g. #37568 and #45521).

We can do better than this. Rethinking our approach to styled content allows us to do away with this class of bugs/headaches altogether, and gain a number of shiny new features at the same time.

Hy hope is that the capabilities introduced by this PR will lead to a much more robust approach to string styling, and by making it easier to produce well-styled content even more beautiful REPL experiences across the board.

## Rundown of changes

An `StyledString` type is introduced that effectively sits on top of other string types. It wraps another `AbstractString` but then separately stores a list of attributes applied to particular regions of the wrapped string. Regions are represented by a `UnitRange{Int}`, and attributes as a `Pair{Symbol, Any}`.

This effectively creates "content" and "attributes" layers, allowing both to be handled much better (conveniently and robustly) than when mixed together. Attributes can be *`Any`thing*, which allows for more than just styling information, e.g. hyperlinks, or other data which makes sense to attach to a region of a string like source location information. Really `StyledString` should be called `PropertizedString`, but I think that's much less catchy 😛.

An `StyledChar` type is also added, which is a mirror of `AbstractString`, just wrapping an `AbstractChar` instead.

To handle styling information, a new type is added to contain all possibly relevant styling information — `Face`. This is very much inspired by https://www.gnu.org/software/emacs/manual/html_node/elisp/Faces.html, as I have found this system to work rather well in practice (why invent a new approach when there's a lovely battle-tested one we can be inspired by? 😉).

The main way faces work is by having a global `Dics{Symbol, Face}` of face names. This has a number of advantages:
- It makes it easy to reference faces by their name
- Packages can easily add new faces
- It makes it possible for faces to inherit from other faces (e.g. have a `:julia_help_prompt` face that inherits from `:julia_prompt`)

Conversion functions to accept and load face specifications from a `Dict{String, Any}` are also added. By loading `~/.julia/config/faces.toml` this resolves #41435.

To handle printing faces well, we need to inquire about the terminal a fair bit more, and so I've replaced `tcap` with a terminfo parser implemented in accordance with `term(5)`.

Lastly, we have a string macro (`@S_str`) that was rather nasty to write, but is very convenient.

#### Currently unimplemented

- `replace`
- property removal

## Usage examples

The example from the `@S_str` docs

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235492934-d3299633-36fe-4a21-8321-83e24d8c4a6f.png)

Easy nested styling

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235493196-753f50e4-3acc-4821-9146-8c1aef5e6cd1.png)

No more issues with unterminated ANSI codes

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235494848-eccefbda-f943-4c1d-85a0-dbd2297b9217.png)

String functions operate on the unstyled content, while preserving styling where possible

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496767-1d537db9-befc-4ee6-b482-e91778e32ceb.png)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496290-d3b6bb79-9c33-4dce-bf6f-b20d696cc5b2.png)

Styled text will actually automatically fall back to the un-styled form when printed to an `io` with `:color != true`.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235496566-330a621d-e0d9-4b36-8714-e9d225e89ef7.png)

Incrementally styling a string, and changing face definitions on-the-fly (note: this uses currently "private API" functions)

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/20903656/235499614-ce8d5aca-a561-4280-979e-d6c176efd1df.png)

## Fancier usage examples

The result of the example "banner using `S"..."`" commit

![image](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/assets/20903656/1e5cdf8a-4e3e-48ef-ae94-a0850c484270)

A fancier `^R` implementation, that's been implemented using `StyledString`s and `JuliaSyntax` that would be a massive pain to do via `printstyled` et al.

(Note: there's a Mac-specific version 3 posts down)

```julia
using BenchmarkTools, Random, VectorizationBase
using LinearAlgebra, LinearSolve, MKL_jll
nc = min(Int(VectorizationBase.num_cores()), Threads.nthreads())
BLAS.set_num_threads(nc)
BenchmarkTools.DEFAULT_PARAMETERS.seconds = 0.5

function luflop(m, n = m; innerflop = 2)
    sum(1:min(m, n)) do k
        invflop = 1
        scaleflop = isempty((k + 1):m) ? 0 : sum((k + 1):m)
        updateflop = isempty((k + 1):n) ? 0 :
                     sum((k + 1):n) do j
            isempty((k + 1):m) ? 0 : sum((k + 1):m) do i
                innerflop
            end
        end
        invflop + scaleflop + updateflop
    end
end

algs = [LUFactorization(), GenericLUFactorization(), RFLUFactorization(), MKLLUFactorization(), FastLUFactorization(), SimpleLUFactorization()]
res = [Float64[] for i in 1:length(algs)]

ns = 4:8:500
for i in 1:length(ns)
    n = ns[i]
    @info "$n × $n"
    rng = MersenneTwister(123)
    global A = rand(rng, n, n)
    global b = rand(rng, n)
    global u0= rand(rng, n)
    
    for j in 1:length(algs)
        bt = @belapsed solve(prob, $(algs[j])).u setup=(prob = LinearProblem(copy(A), copy(b); u0 = copy(u0), alias_A=true, alias_b=true))
        push!(res[j], luflop(n) / bt / 1e9)
    end
end

using Plots
__parameterless_type(T) = Base.typename(T).wrapper
parameterless_type(x) = __parameterless_type(typeof(x))
parameterless_type(::Type{T}) where {T} = __parameterless_type(T)

p = plot(ns, res[1]; ylabel = "GFLOPs", xlabel = "N", title = "GFLOPs for NxN LU Factorization", label = string(Symbol(parameterless_type(algs[1]))), legend=:outertopright)
for i in 2:length(res)
    plot!(p, ns, res[i]; label = string(Symbol(parameterless_type(algs[i]))))
end
p

savefig("lubench.png")
savefig("lubench.pdf")
```

[lubench.pdf](https://github.com/SciML/LinearSolve.jl/files/12287070/lubench.pdf)
![lubench](https://github.com/SciML/LinearSolve.jl/assets/1814174/84d091a9-f9f6-4e5e-a208-0a1c65365cec)

(Note: there's a Mac-specific version 3 posts down)

```julia
using BenchmarkTools, Random, VectorizationBase
using LinearAlgebra, LinearSolve, MKL_jll
nc = min(Int(VectorizationBase.num_cores()), Threads.nthreads())
BLAS.set_num_threads(nc)
BenchmarkTools.DEFAULT_PARAMETERS.seconds = 0.5

function luflop(m, n = m; innerflop = 2)
    sum(1:min(m, n)) do k
        invflop = 1
        scaleflop = isempty((k + 1):m) ? 0 : sum((k + 1):m)
        updateflop = isempty((k + 1):n) ? 0 :
                     sum((k + 1):n) do j
            isempty((k + 1):m) ? 0 : sum((k + 1):m) do i
                innerflop
            end
        end
        invflop + scaleflop + updateflop
    end
end

algs = [LUFactorization(), GenericLUFactorization(), RFLUFactorization(), MKLLUFactorization(), FastLUFactorization(), SimpleLUFactorization()]
res = [Float64[] for i in 1:length(algs)]

ns = 4:8:500
for i in 1:length(ns)
    n = ns[i]
    @info "$n × $n"
    rng = MersenneTwister(123)
    global A = rand(rng, n, n)
    global b = rand(rng, n)
    global u0= rand(rng, n)
    
    for j in 1:length(algs)
        bt = @belapsed solve(prob, $(algs[j])).u setup=(prob = LinearProblem(copy(A), copy(b); u0 = copy(u0), alias_A=true, alias_b=true))
        push!(res[j], luflop(n) / bt / 1e9)
    end
end

using Plots
__parameterless_type(T) = Base.typename(T).wrapper
parameterless_type(x) = __parameterless_type(typeof(x))
parameterless_type(::Type{T}) where {T} = __parameterless_type(T)

p = plot(ns, res[1]; ylabel = "GFLOPs", xlabel = "N", title = "GFLOPs for NxN LU Factorization", label = string(Symbol(parameterless_type(algs[1]))), legend=:outertopright)
for i in 2:length(res)
    plot!(p, ns, res[i]; label = string(Symbol(parameterless_type(algs[i]))))
end
p

savefig("lubench.png")
savefig("lubench.pdf")
```

 big(z::Complex{T}) where {T<:Real} = Complex{big(T)}(z)
 
 ## Array operations on complex numbers ##
+"""
+    complex(A::AbstractArray)
+
+Return an array containing the complex analog of each entry in array `A`.
+
+Equivalent to `complex.(A)`, except that the return value may alias all or part
+of `A` in accordance with the behavior of `convert(T, A)` given output type `T`.
 
+# Examples

- M1 MacOS
- Raspberry Pi 4 RPi OS
- Ryzen 3700X Linux
- Ryzen 3700X Windows
- i9-12900 Linux

# run in a fresh session
@show typeof([1, missing, 2])
```
prints `typeof([1, missing, 2]) = Vector{Union{Missing, Int64}}` as expected.
But add a totally irrelevant line, and eltype changes:
```julia
# run in a fresh session
[1, missing, nothing, 2]
@show typeof([1, missing, 2])
```
prints `typeof([1, missing, 2]) = Vector{Any}`.

Only happens on 1.9rc, not 1.8 nor 1.9 betas.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48961/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48961/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1464062507","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48961#issuecomment-1464062507","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/48961","id":1464062507,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5XQ9Ir","user":{"login":"christiangnrd","id":28689358,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI4Njg5MzU4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/28689358?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd","html_url":"https://github.com/christiangnrd","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/christiangnrd/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-03-10T16:32:19Z","updated_at":"2023-03-10T16:32:19Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"```

noticed that requesting a large `Vector{UInt8}` from another local worker is much slower in Julia 1.9. The example below went from **14ms** to **96ms**.

Julia 1.8.3:

```julia
julia> using Distributed

julia> using BenchmarkTools

julia> p = Distributed.addprocs(1)[1]
2

julia> ex = quote
                   zeros(UInt8, 50_000_000)
               end;

julia> f2() = Distributed.remotecall_eval(Main, p, ex);

julia> f2()

My results looked more like gbaraldi's and Kristoffer's than the 6x slowdown, but I did notice that while I get pretty much identical median times (30ms) in both versions, the "good" one has faster minimums but higher maximums, and the histogram usually has 3 "humps", while the "bad" version has much more consistent times but a slightly slower minimum time.

That said, here's what my attempt at a bisect returned.
